title: Compilers: Principles, Techniques & Tools
subtitle: The Dragon Book — The Definitive Textbook for Compiler Construction
author: Alfred V. Aho, Monica S. Lam, Ravi Sethi, Jeffrey D. Ullman
themes: Formal language theory as the mathematical foundation of compiler design
The complete compiler pipeline from source code to machine code
Lexical analysis via regular expressions and finite automata (Thompson NFA construction)
Parsing theory: LL, LR, SLR, LALR with correctness proofs
Syntax-directed translation as the bridge between parsing and code generation
Intermediate code representations: syntax trees, three-address code, DAGs
Runtime systems: stack allocation, heap management, garbage collection theory
Machine-independent optimization via dataflow analysis framework
The interplay between compiler theory and modern hardware (ILP, multicore)
excerpt: Compilers: Principles, Techniques, and Tools (the Dragon Book) is the definitive textbook on compiler construction. First published in 1986 and updated in 2006 with three new chapters on parallelism and locality, it provides the complete theoretical foundation — from lexical analysis through interprocedural optimization — that every language implementer must master.
awards: ACM A.M. Turing Award (Aho and Ullman, 2020) — partial basis
Most widely used compiler textbook worldwide
rationale: Root book #1 for compilers-and-language-runtimes. The Dragon Book is mandatory inclusion — no compiler library is complete without it. This 2006 2nd edition (purple dragon) is the current authoritative version, with Monica Lam as new co-author and chapters on ILP and interprocedural analysis added.

relatedBooks.title: Engineering a Compiler
relatedBooks.connection: Practical complement; less mathematical, more implementation-focused

relatedBooks.title: Modern Compiler Implementation in C/ML/Java
relatedBooks.connection: Hands-on implementation using Tiger language; pairs as a practical follow-on

relatedBooks.title: Programming Language Pragmatics
relatedBooks.connection: Broader PL + runtime coverage; reader-friendly alternative for parsing chapter

relatedBooks.title: Crafting Interpreters
relatedBooks.connection: Accessible companion for the front-end chapters; builds an actual interpreter
